module.title: EQ & Frequency
module.description: Go beyond basic EQ moves — understand the frequency spectrum deeply, fix problem frequencies surgically, shape tonal character, separate competing instruments, and use mid-side and dynamic EQ techniques.

lessons.title: How Frequency Works in a Mix
lessons.body:
EQ decisions made without understanding the frequency spectrum are guesswork. Once you internalise what lives where — which instruments own which ranges, where mud collects, where clarity lives — every EQ move becomes intentional. This lesson maps the spectrum and explains why mixes get cluttered.

## The Audible Spectrum

Human hearing spans roughly 20 Hz to 20 kHz — about ten octaves. Each octave doubles in frequency. The middle of the piano keyboard sits at A4 = 440 Hz. The deepest bass notes a subwoofer produces sit around 20–40 Hz. The shimmer of a cymbal extends past 10 kHz.

Producers divide this range into bands with distinct sonic characters:

## The Frequency Bands

**Sub Bass — 20 to 60 Hz**
Felt more than heard. The fundamental of a well-tuned 808 sits around 40–50 Hz. Kick drum sub energy is in this range. On small speakers these frequencies are inaudible; on subwoofers they provide the physical impact of a track.

*What lives here*: 808 fundamentals, kick sub, synthesised bass fundamentals in certain genres.
*Problem*: Too much energy here makes a mix sound heavy on big speakers and completely empty on small ones. Ruthlessly high-pass everything that does not need sub energy.

**Bass — 60 to 250 Hz**
Warmth, body, and weight. The fundamental notes of a bass guitar or bass synth live here. The kick drum body (the thud you hear, not just the sub) is in this range. This is also where most low-end mud accumulates in dense mixes.

*What lives here*: Bass/808 body, kick body, low piano notes, cello fundamentals.
*Problem*: Multiple instruments stacking low-end creates a blurry, indistinct bottom — the primary cause of muddy mixes.

**Low Mids — 250 to 500 Hz**
The muddiness zone. Almost every instrument has some energy here, and it piles up fast. Guitars and pianos have boxy resonances here. Drum rooms accumulate here. Excessive low-mid energy makes a mix sound thick and dark — not warm, just congested.

*What lives here*: Lower harmonics of almost all instruments.
*Problem*: The most common source of mix mud. Small cuts here (2–4 dB) on multiple tracks make the entire mix clearer.

**Mids — 500 Hz to 2 kHz**
Presence, body, and intelligibility. The human voice is centred here. Guitar and synth fundamental harmonics are prominent in this range. This is where most musical information lives — melody, rhythm, and harmony are all dominated by the mid frequencies.

*What lives here*: Vocals, guitars, synth harmonics, snare body, piano mid-range.
*Problem*: Harshness and nasality if over-boosted. A mix that sounds thin and lacking body is usually missing energy in the upper portion of this range.

**Upper Mids — 2 to 6 kHz**
Clarity, attack, and edge. This range determines how forward and present an element sounds. The click of a kick drum, the snap of a snare, the consonants of speech — all defined here. This is also the harshness zone: boosting without care creates ear fatigue.

*What lives here*: Transient attacks of drums, vocal consonants, guitar pick attack, synth bite.
*Problem*: Boosting 2–4 kHz aggressively sounds exciting for 10 minutes, then becomes fatiguing. Subtle boosts; larger cuts.

**Presence — 4 to 10 kHz**
Definition, sibilance, and cut-through. This is where lead elements separate from the mix. A presence boost makes a vocal or lead synth cut through without raising its volume. It is also where sibilance (harsh S and T sounds) lives in vocals.

*What lives here*: Vocal sibilance, hi-hat body, synth brightness, string harmonics.
*Problem*: Over-boosted presence sounds thin and digital. Sibilance in this range causes fatigue.

**Air — 10 to 20 kHz**
Shimmer, sparkle, openness. High-shelf boosts here add perceived openness and space to a mix. Most content in this range is harmonics and noise rather than fundamental frequencies.

*What lives here*: Cymbal shimmer, room ambience, synthesised air.
*Problem*: Too much boosting here makes a mix sound harsh and brittle, especially on compressed streaming formats.

## Frequency Masking: Why Mixes Get Muddy

Masking occurs when two sounds occupy the same frequency range at similar loudness — the louder one partially or completely obscures the quieter one. The listener perceives both as a blurred, indistinct wash rather than two separate elements.

**Classic masking example**: A bass synth with a prominent fundamental at 100 Hz and a kick drum with its body at 100 Hz. Both are audible in solo. Together, the lower-energy one disappears into the louder one. The solution is not to raise the quieter element — it is to carve EQ space so they occupy slightly different frequency zones.

Masking is frequency-specific: two elements mask each other at the frequencies where they overlap. They can coexist cleanly at frequencies where only one is present. This is the underlying logic behind EQ for separation, covered in Lesson 4.

## Reading the Spectrum Analyser

Parametric EQ 2 includes a real-time spectrum analyser. Enable it by right-clicking the display. Use it to:

- Identify where an instrument's energy is concentrated
- Spot unexpected resonances (narrow peaks that stick above their neighbours)
- Verify that high-pass filters are working (the left side should fall off)
- See how much low-mid energy is accumulating on non-bass tracks

A critical skill: learn to listen for what you see. When you see a spike at 350 Hz on a chord pad and simultaneously hear the boxy quality of that pad, you are building the frequency-to-sound mapping that makes EQ decisions fast and accurate.
keyTakeaways: The low-mids (250–500 Hz) are the primary source of mix mud — almost every instrument has energy here, and small cuts of 2–4 dB on multiple non-bass tracks clear the mix more than any other single move.
Frequency masking is why elements that sound clear in solo disappear in the mix — when two sounds share the same frequency range at similar levels, the louder one obscures the quieter one.

Learn to connect what you see in the spectrum analyser to what you hear: when a boxy quality coincides with a spike at 350 Hz, you are building the ear-training that makes every future EQ decision faster.
lessons.tryThis: Open a project with at least a kick, bass, and chord pad. Solo the chord pad and enable the Parametric EQ 2 spectrum analyser. Note where most of the energy is — it is likely concentrated between 100 and 500 Hz. Now unsolo and listen to the full mix. Add a high-pass filter at 250 Hz on the chord pad. Compare before and after in the context of the full mix — the pad has not lost its character, but the low end of the mix is suddenly cleaner. This single high-pass cut is one of the highest-impact EQ moves in any dense mix.

lessons.title: Surgical EQ: Fixing Problem Frequencies
lessons.body:
Surgical EQ means making narrow, targeted cuts to remove specific problems — resonances, harshness, muddiness — without affecting the surrounding frequencies. It is the first pass of any EQ session: fix what is wrong before you consider what might sound better.

## Surgical vs Tonal EQ

**Surgical EQ** uses high-Q (narrow) cuts to remove problems. The goal is transparency — the listener should not hear the EQ, only the absence of the problem.

**Tonal EQ** uses low-Q (broad) boosts and cuts to shape the overall character and colour of a sound. This is covered in Lesson 3.

Always do surgical work first. Tonal shaping applied over an unaddressed resonance just makes the resonance sound more coloured.

## The Sweep Technique (Detailed)

The sweep technique locates resonances you can hear but cannot pinpoint:

1. Add a peak band in Parametric EQ 2 with a very high Q (narrow — around 8–12) and a large boost of +12 to +15 dB
2. Solo the track and play it in a loop
3. Drag the frequency slowly across the range where you suspect the problem
4. When the annoying quality becomes grotesquely exaggerated, you have found the resonant frequency
5. Switch the boost to a cut of −4 to −8 dB
6. Reduce the Q to a moderate width (3–5) — the cut should affect the resonance and a little of its neighbours, not a razor-thin slice
7. Adjust the gain until the problem is controlled but the sound still has body

**Important**: Always check the cut in the context of the full mix, not solo. A cut that sounds perfect in solo may be inaudible in the mix — or may have removed something that was contributing to the mix's overall warmth.

## Common Problems and Their Frequencies

**Muddiness / Boominess — 100 to 350 Hz**
Sounds thick, unclear, indistinct. Low-end elements blend together and lose definition. Common on mixes with many instruments stacking low-mid energy.
*Fix*: High-pass non-bass tracks. Add a moderate cut (−2 to −4 dB, Q of 1–2) centred around 200–300 Hz on the most offending elements.

**Boxy / Cardboard — 300 to 600 Hz**
Sounds hollow and enclosed, like audio played in a small box. Common on drum samples recorded in small rooms, cheap keyboards, certain VST presets.
*Fix*: Sweep between 300–600 Hz. Cut 4–6 dB with moderate Q (3–5). Snares often benefit enormously from a cut around 500 Hz.

**Honky / Nasal — 600 Hz to 1.2 kHz**
Sounds like audio played through a phone or a megaphone. Common on guitar amp simulations, certain piano samples, mid-heavy synthesisers.
*Fix*: Sweep between 600 Hz and 1.2 kHz. Narrow cut of 3–6 dB.

**Harsh / Edgy — 2 to 4 kHz**
Sounds aggressive and fatiguing after extended listening. Common on overdriven synthesisers, highly compressed audio, low-quality samples.
*Fix*: A moderate cut at the offending frequency. This range is tricky — too much cutting removes the presence and attack that make elements audible in the mix. Cut conservatively (2–4 dB) and listen carefully.

**Sibilance — 5 to 10 kHz**
The harsh S, T, and SH sounds in vocals and certain synth patches. Specific to the attack of transients in this range.
*Fix*: A narrow cut exactly at the sibilant peak (use the sweep technique). Or use a de-esser, which is a dynamic version of the same cut.

**Hiss / Noise Floor — 8 kHz and above**
Constant high-frequency noise from microphones, audio interfaces, or sample recordings. Not a resonance — a broadband noise floor.
*Fix*: A low-pass filter set just above the highest musically relevant frequency in the recording. Or a gentle high-shelf cut (−3 to −6 dB) above 10–12 kHz.

## How Much to Cut

The right amount of cut is the minimum that solves the problem. These are starting points:

| Problem Severity | Cut Amount | Q |
|---|---|---|
| Subtle resonance | −3 to −4 dB | 3–5 |
| Moderate resonance | −5 to −8 dB | 4–6 |
| Severe resonance | −8 to −12 dB | 5–8 |

Narrow Q for surgical work. Widen the Q slightly if you want the cut to affect a broader area.

## The Context Rule

Every surgical cut should be auditioned in the full mix, not in solo. A resonance that is extremely annoying in solo may be completely inaudible in the context of a full mix — cutting it wastes processing and potentially removes useful harmonic content. Conversely, a problem that seems subtle in solo may be causing serious masking in the mix.

The test: make the cut, toggle the EQ band off and on while the full mix plays. If you cannot hear a difference in the mix, the cut is doing nothing — remove it.
keyTakeaways: Surgical EQ always comes before tonal EQ — remove problems first, then shape character. Colouring a sound that still has a resonance just makes the resonance more prominent.
Use the sweep technique: narrow high-Q boost (+12 dB, Q of 8–12), drag slowly until the problem is exaggerated, then flip to a moderate cut (−4 to −8 dB) and widen the Q slightly.
Always A/B the cut in the full mix, not solo — if toggling the band on and off produces no audible difference in the mix context, the cut is doing nothing and should be removed.
lessons.tryThis: Pick the most problematic-sounding element in your current mix — the one that feels harsh, boxy, or muddy. Add Parametric EQ 2 and set a peak band to +12 dB with Q at 10. Play the track in a loop and drag the frequency slowly between 200 Hz and 3 kHz until the worst quality of the sound becomes grotesque. Note the exact frequency on the display. Switch the gain to −6 dB and set Q to 4. Now toggle the band on and off in the full mix — if you hear a clear improvement, keep the cut and adjust its depth. If you cannot hear a difference in context, remove the band entirely.
